steps.title: Identify what the finding shows
steps.content: Lepofsky et al. found that the practice described in Kwakwaka'wakw songs — building terraced clam gardens — corresponded with larger, more abundant clams. The songs preserved an effective ecological method. The correct answer must reflect that specific finding without adding unsupported claims.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — "representation of practical applications of ecological knowledge is the defining characteristic of the music of certain Indigenous peoples.": Overreaches — the passage gives examples of ecological knowledge in songs but never claims it is a "defining characteristic" of the music. ✗
  • Choice B — "the Kwakwaka'wakw people likely would not have detailed their creation of clam gardens in songs if their efforts had not produced significantly larger clams.": Speculates about motivation for composing the songs, which the passage never addresses. ✗
  • Choice C — "the clams harvested from intertidal terraces by Kwakwaka'wakw people in the past likely were a different species than the clams found in those areas today.": Invents a species-change claim not mentioned anywhere in the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — "effective methods for the cultivation of sources of sustenance are among the ecological knowledge preserved in Indigenous songs.": Correctly generalizes the finding: the songs preserved a working cultivation method, which is a type of ecological knowledge. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
